intitule: Human Rights and Gender Equality: Vocabulary Recognition (Ressource)

enonce.texte:
Match each term on the left with its correct definition on the right. Write the letter of the definition next to the term.

  • 1. Human Rights ___
  • 2. Discrimination ___
  • 3. Gender Equality ___
  • 4. Empowerment ___
  • 5. Stereotype ___

Definitions:
A. Unfair treatment of a person or group based on prejudice.
B. The state of having the same rights, opportunities, and responsibilities regardless of being male or female.
C. Basic rights and freedoms that belong to every person in the world.
D. A fixed, often incorrect, idea about what a particular type of person is like.
E. The process of becoming stronger and more confident in controlling one's life and claiming one's rights.

questions.texte: Match the terms to their definitions.

enonce.texte:
Read each scenario below. Identify which human right or principle of gender equality is being violated. Choose from the following list: Right to Education, Right to Non-Discrimination, Right to Freedom from Violence, Right to Equal Pay, Right to Participation in Government.

  1. In a village, parents refuse to send their daughter to school because they believe only boys need an education.
  2. A female manager discovers she is paid 30% less than her male colleague who has the same job and experience.
  3. During an election, women are turned away from voting because 'politics is for men'.
  4. A husband regularly beats his wife when she disagrees with him.
  5. A company refuses to hire a qualified candidate because of his ethnic background.

questions.texte: Identify the right violated in each scenario.
questions.reponse: 1. Right to Education; 2. Right to Equal Pay; 3. Right to Participation in Government; 4. Right to Freedom from Violence; 5. Right to Non-Discrimination

enonce.texte:
Complete each sentence with the correct word from the box. You may need to change the form of the word (e.g., plural or verb tense).

Word Box: equality, discrimination, rights, empowerment, privileges, gender, stereotypes, abuse

  1. The constitution guarantees fundamental human _______ to all citizens.
  2. _______ based on disability is illegal in many countries.
  3. Microfinance programs for women aim at economic _______.
  4. _______ roles are social constructs that vary from one culture to another.
  5. We must fight against harmful _______ that limit children's potential.
  6. Child _______ in all its forms (physical, emotional, sexual) must be reported.
  7. Men and women should enjoy the same _______ in the workplace.
  8. True _______ between genders requires changing mentalities and laws.

questions.texte: Fill in the blanks with appropriate vocabulary words.
questions.reponse: 1. rights; 2. Discrimination; 3. empowerment; 4. Gender; 5. stereotypes; 6. abuse; 7. privileges; 8. equality

intitule: Analyzing a Case Study on Gender Discrimination (Competence)

enonce.texte:
Read the case study carefully and answer the questions that follow.

Case Study: The School of Two Worlds

In the village of Nkolbisson, the public secondary school has 500 boys and only 150 girls. The principal, Mr. Atangana, explains: 'Girls often drop out after class 4 because parents prefer to invest in boys' education. Also, when girls reach puberty, many are forced into early marriage.' A local NGO, 'Equal Future', wants to intervene. They propose: (1) building separate sanitary facilities for girls, (2) creating a scholarship program for top-performing girls, (3) training teachers to avoid gender bias in the classroom, and (4) organizing awareness sessions for parents on the importance of girls' education. However, some villagers oppose the project, saying it gives 'special privileges' to girls and discriminates against boys.

questions.texte: Identify two human rights violations mentioned in the case study.
questions.reponse: 1. Right to Education (girls dropping out). 2. Right to Freedom from Forced/Early Marriage.

questions.texte: Explain why the principal's observation about parents 'preferring to invest in boys' education' is a form of gender discrimination.
questions.reponse: It is discrimination because it involves treating individuals (girls) unfavorably based on their gender. It denies girls the same opportunity for education and future economic independence that boys receive, perpetuating inequality.

questions.texte: Analyze the four proposals from the NGO 'Equal Future'. For each proposal, state whether it promotes gender equality or creates reverse discrimination. Justify your answer.
questions.reponse: 1. Separate sanitary facilities: Promotes equality because it addresses a specific need (privacy, hygiene) that keeps girls in school. Not discrimination, but accommodation of a biological difference. 2. Scholarship for top-performing girls: Promotes equality because it counteracts existing societal barriers and encourages girls to stay in school. Affirmative action to level the playing field. 3. Teacher training on gender bias: Promotes equality because it removes subtle discrimination in the classroom (e.g., calling on boys more often). 4. Awareness sessions for parents: Promotes equality by changing the underlying cultural beliefs that cause discrimination. None of these are reverse discrimination because they do not take away existing rights from boys; they only extend rights to girls who were previously excluded.

questions.texte: Propose two additional actions that the school could take to promote gender equality without excluding boys.
questions.reponse: 1. Introduce a 'Gender Club' where boys and girls discuss stereotypes and plan joint activities. 2. Revise the curriculum to include examples of female scientists, leaders, and artists to provide role models for all students. 3. Implement a mentorship program where older girls mentor younger girls, and older boys mentor younger boys on respectful relationships and academic success.

enonce.texte:
You are a senior prefect at your school. You have observed that female students are not allowed to run for the position of 'Head Boy' (the highest student leadership role). Instead, they can only run for 'Head Girl' which has fewer responsibilities and less interaction with school administration. The school principal says this is 'tradition'.

Write a formal letter to the principal (approximately 250-300 words) in which you:

  • State the facts of the situation.
  • Explain why this policy violates principles of gender equality and human rights (refer to specific vocabulary: discrimination, equality, empowerment, stereotypes).
  • Propose a fair alternative (e.g., gender-neutral positions, joint leadership).
  • Request a meeting to discuss the issue.

questions.texte: Write a formal letter to the principal as described.
questions.reponse: Rubric for evaluation: Content (states facts, uses vocabulary correctly: discrimination, equality, empowerment, stereotypes); Structure (formal letter format: address, date, subject, salutation, body, closing); Language (correct grammar, punctuation, spelling); Argumentation (logical, respectful but firm, proposes concrete alternative). Sample letter content: 'Dear Principal, I am writing to respectfully bring to your attention a policy that perpetuates gender discrimination... Excluding female students from the Head Boy position based solely on gender reinforces harmful stereotypes that leadership is male... This violates the principle of gender equality and denies girls the right to equal participation... I propose creating two gender-neutral positions of Student Body President and Vice President, open to all students regardless of gender... I request a meeting to discuss this further...'

enonce.texte:
Your school is celebrating 'Human Rights Week'. You are asked to design a poster to raise awareness about gender equality among students.

Instructions: Describe in detail the poster you would create. Your description must include:

  • A catchy title or slogan.
  • A description of the images/illustrations you would use (at least two).
  • Three key messages written in complete sentences using at least 5 vocabulary words from Lesson 28 (rights, discrimination, equality, empowerment, gender, stereotypes, privileges, abuse).
  • The colors you would use and why.

questions.texte: Describe your awareness poster for gender equality.
questions.reponse: Example: Title: 'Equal Rights, Equal Opportunities: End Gender Discrimination Now!' Images: (1) A split image showing a boy studying engineering and a girl studying medicine, breaking stereotypes. (2) A group of diverse students (boys and girls) holding hands in a circle. Key messages: 'Gender equality means everyone enjoys the same fundamental rights, regardless of being male or female.' 'We must challenge stereotypes that limit our potential and create discrimination.' 'Empowerment of all students leads to a stronger, more just community for everyone.' Colors: Purple (color of gender equality movement), blue and pink together (symbolizing unity of genders), and white (for peace and fairness).
